The algorithm iterates a while loop for the desired peak hours duration (line 2). In each iteration, the algorithm computes for each appliance (line 3) and its corresponding thresholds (i.e.) in a given environment (line 4). Afterwards, it computes the overall load of appliances in on-state at time (line 5) along with the userstate based on the computed load (line 6). Then, the algorithm passes the user-states to Algorithm 1 that returns (line 7), which is used to get average probability of consuming maximum power in remaining peak load hours. Next, appliances in on-state are selected (line 8). For each selected appliance (line 9), the algorithm first obtains the userdefined on-off threshold for the appliance (line 10). The subsequent steps (lines 11 to line 13), are executed for these appliances if the user defined threshold is greater than the threshold of human comfort for the appliance. Therein, it computes the difference (denoted by) between the user-defined threshold () and minimum threshold required for human comfort () (line 12). The threshold value is updated based on this difference () (line 13). Algorithm 2: PF-PEC Algorithm Input: Human comfort constraints and parameters,; peak hours duration Output:,, 1: ← 0 2: while ∈ do 3: Compute for all type of loads 4: Compute for all type of loads 5: Compute load in on-state at 6: Find user state from computed load 7: Invoke Algorithm 1 and save value of 8: ← Appliances in on-state at 9: for each appliance in do 10: Obtain user defined on-off thresholds () of the appliance 11: if > then; 12: = - 13: = + × 14: end if 15: end for 16: + + 17: end while 18: return

The numerical results are illustrated in Table 3a and 3b. In that respect, different KWh ranges are specified that corresponds to the potential combination of outcomes considered by Algorithm 1. The predicted power consumption is provided in terms of the probability of consuming energy in a particular KWh range for different time slots. The values in Table 3a and 3b are not specific to a single user. These probabilistic values represent the likelihood of all users cumulatively consuming a certain power. The real energy consumed (KWh) at each time slot is also reported in the table and can be compared with the KWh range of highest probability event. For example, in 3a for time slot 8am-9am, the actual energy consumed is 0.01 KWh whereas the corresponding predicted KWh range, i.e. 0.01-0.03, has the highest probability. Similarly, for other time slots, the results obtained from our energy consumption prediction model in terms of KWh ranges and their corresponding probabilities fairly match with that of actual energy consumption of the smart home. In Fig. 7, the percentage similarity Monday 6 h,3 m,9 s 6 h,3 m,9 s 6 h,3 m 5 h,49 m 5 h,49 m 5 h,58 m 4 h,54 m 4 h,54 m 5 h,45 m Tuesday 6 h,42 m 6 h,42 m 6 h,42 m 6 h 6 h 6 h 4 h,1 m 3 h,51 m 5 h,46 m Wednesday 7 h,32 m 7 h,32 m 7 h,32 m 5 h,50 m 5 h,50 m 6 h,1 m 3 h,18 m 1 h,18 m 5 h,37 m Thursday 6 h,49 m 6 h,49 m 6 h,49 m 6 h,27 m 6 h,27 m 6 h,38 m 3 h,35 m 35 m 6 h,22 m Friday 7 h,5 m 7 h,5 m 7 h,5 m 5 h,33 m 5 h,33 m 7 h,5 m 3 h56 m 6 m 6 h,48 m of predicted energy consumption with that of actual is shown on axis and the events probabilities are shown on -axis. This similarity measure is simply the percentage number of predictions matching with actual power consumption. It shows that the highest probability events in 3a and 3b have more chances to correctly predict the actual energy usage which in turn means that the actual energy consumed have more chances of falling in the KWh range of highest probability event.
